---
title: "Distributional regression with drmTMB"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Distributional regression with drmTMB}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

`drmTMB` is being developed as a model-first package: the documentation should
pair symbolic equations with the R syntax that fits them. The current
implementation supports Gaussian location-scale models with fixed effects and
optional random effects in the location formula and residual-scale random
intercepts in the `sigma` formula. It also supports a first group-level
random-effect scale grammar such as `sd(id) ~ x_group` for one or more
distinct unlabelled Gaussian `mu` random intercepts:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(y ~ x1 + (1 | id) + (0 + x1 | id), sigma ~ x1),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

The ordinary correlated random intercept-slope form is also implemented for
univariate Gaussian `mu`:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(y ~ x1 + (1 + x1 | id), sigma ~ x1),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

The intercept-slope correlation from this block is a group-level correlation
reported under `corpars$mu`; it is not residual bivariate `rho12`.

A labelled version is implemented for univariate Gaussian `mu`:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(y ~ x1 + (1 + x1 | p | id), sigma ~ x1),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

The middle label `p` is retained in group-level SD and correlation names. It
does not yet share covariance across `sigma`, `mu1`, or `mu2` formulas.

Residual-scale random intercepts in `sigma` are also implemented:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(y ~ x1 + (1 | id), sigma ~ x1 + (1 | id)),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

This is a model for residual or within-observation scale. It is distinct from
`sd(id) ~ x_group`, which models the standard deviation of an unlabelled
group-level `mu` random intercept.

The first implemented bivariate seed covers fixed-effect location-coscale
regression with predictor-dependent residual correlation:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2,
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1,
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

The planned double-hierarchical bivariate version adds individual-level random
intercepts and random slopes to each mean formula, following the
individual-differences framework of O'Dea et al. (2022):

```r
drmTMB(
  formula = drm_formula(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2 + (1 + x2 | p | ID),
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1      + (1 + x2 | p | ID),
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

Here `sigma1` and `sigma2` are residual scale parameters. The random-effect
standard deviations and correlations from `(1 + x2 | p | ID)` are separate
group-level quantities.

Meta-analysis uses the ordinary Gaussian family plus a known sampling
covariance marker:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    yi ~ x1 + x2 + meta_known_V(V = V),
    sigma ~ x1
  ),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

For the mathematical details, start with the location-scale and bivariate
coscale tutorials. They spell out which part of the R formula creates each
linear predictor, which scale each parameter uses, and which quantities are
residual scales versus group-level standard deviations.
